name: Generalized Algebraic Data Types
summary: Constructors return refined instantiations of a type constructor. Pattern matching on a GADT introduces local type equalities, which must be available while checking the branch. Powerful for typed embedded languages, length-indexed data, state machines, and proof-carrying APIs; complicates inference because pattern matching introduces local equality assumptions that don't leak outside the branch.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Generalized algebraic data types allow constructors to return refined instantiations of a type constructor. The classical example is a typed expression tree where `Lit Int : Expr Int`, `Add : Expr Int -> Expr Int -> Expr Int`, and `If : Expr Bool -> Expr a -> Expr a -> Expr a` — pattern matching on an `Expr Int` knows the result type is `Int`.

Pattern matching on a GADT introduces local type equalities, which must be available while checking the branch. GADTs are powerful for typed embedded languages, length-indexed data, state machines, and proof-carrying APIs, but they require careful inference design to avoid ambiguous or unsound conclusions.

sections.title: Why GADTs complicate inference
sections.content:
The fundamental issue: a GADT pattern match introduces a *local* type equality (e.g., "in this branch, `a ~ Int`"). If inference were allowed to use this equality to bind outer type variables, principality breaks: the same expression could have different inferred types depending on which branch the inferer happened to traverse first.

OutsideIn(X) (`data/constraint-based-typing#outsidein-x`) is the canonical solution: the inference engine has an outer zone (no local equalities) and an inner zone (local GADT equalities active). Variables created in the outer zone are *rigid* in the inner zone — they cannot be bound by inner equalities; they only act as known-but-unrefined type witnesses.

The practical consequence is that GADT-using code must annotate more than ordinary ADT code: the function whose body matches on a GADT typically needs an explicit return type so the inference engine knows what the outer zone expects.
